report:
## Growth

Both KO and PEP have posted steady top-line growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, though at different scales and rates. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], representing cumulative growth of roughly 11.5% over three years, with each year adding incrementally: $45.8B in FY2023 [KO:Q1], $47.1B in FY2024 [KO:Q2], and $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3]. PEP, operating at roughly twice KO's revenue base, grew from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], a cumulative gain of about 8.7%, with the bulk of that growth front-loaded in FY2023 ($91.5B [PEP:Q1]) and only modest incremental gains in FY2024 ($91.9B [PEP:Q2]) and FY2025 ($93.9B [PEP:Q3]). In percentage terms, KO has demonstrated slightly stronger compounding momentum across this window, while PEP's growth has decelerated noticeably after FY2023.

**KO:** Coca-Cola has delivered consistent annual revenue growth, rising from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], a cumulative increase of approximately 11.5% with no year showing a decline. Growth has been relatively steady year-over-year, suggesting durable pricing power and volume resilience across its beverage portfolio.

**PEP:** PepsiCo's revenue trajectory shows strong initial growth—jumping from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $91.5B in FY2023 [PEP:Q1]—but has since plateaued, with FY2024 revenues of $91.9B [PEP:Q2] nearly flat versus FY2023 before a modest recovery to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3]. This deceleration suggests PEP may be facing greater headwinds from volume softness or pricing normalization in its diversified food and beverage segments.

---

## Profitability

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge in the most recent year—though important non-recurring items on both sides complicate a straightforward structural comparison. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing.

At the bottom line, KO's net income reached $13.1B [KO:Q7] in FY2025 (a net margin of ~27.3%), while PEP's net income declined to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6] (a net margin of ~8.8%). However, both headline figures are materially influenced by non-recurring items. KO's ~38% YoY surge in operating income—from $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10] to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11]—is atypical for a mature beverage company and includes identifiable one-time gains such as a ~$331M gain on the CCEP stake sale, ~$409M in securities gains, and ~$102M in refranchising gains, collectively exceeding $800M; the sustainability of this rebound is therefore uncertain pending a fuller decomposition of non-recurring items. Similarly, PEP's net income decline should not be read straightforwardly as operational deterioration: PEP's FY2025 results include material non-recurring charges such as a ~$251M Rockstar brand impairment, a ~$19M TBG impairment, ~$50M in merger-related intangible impairments, and additional restructuring charges, which together account for a meaningful portion of the year-over-year shortfall. Adjusting for these items on both sides would likely narrow the apparent profitability gap. That said, KO's structurally higher gross margins—rooted in its concentrate-heavy business model—represent a durable advantage over PEP's more manufacturing-intensive portfolio.

**KO:** Coca-Cola's reported FY2025 profitability metrics are strong, with net income of $13.1B [KO:Q7] and operating income of $13.8B [KO:Q11] on revenues of $47.9B [KO:Q3], and a gross margin of roughly 61.6% [KO:Q15]. However, the sharp YoY operating income increase from $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10] includes over $800M in identifiable non-recurring gains (asset sales, securities, refranchising), meaning the headline improvement overstates the degree of underlying operational progress. KO's structural gross margin advantage over PEP remains intact, but the FY2025 earnings rebound warrants scrutiny before being treated as a new baseline.

**PEP:** PepsiCo's net income declined to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] in FY2025 from $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6], and operating income fell to $11.5B [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B [PEP:Q10], even as revenues grew modestly to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. While this headline deterioration is real, a significant portion reflects non-recurring charges—including brand impairments and restructuring costs totaling several hundred million dollars—rather than purely structural margin erosion. PEP's gross margin of ~54.1% [PEP:Q15] trails KO's considerably, a persistent feature of its diversified snack and beverage model, but the FY2025 net income decline is likely worse than underlying operational trends would suggest on a normalized basis.

---

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge sharply in the most recent year. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

growth.notes: All four revenue figures for KO (FY2022: $43.0B, FY2023: $45.8B, FY2024: $47.1B, FY2025: $47.9B) and PEP (FY2022: $86.4B, FY2023: $91.5B, FY2024: $91.9B, FY2025: $93.9B) are confirmed exactly by the retrieved XBRL data. Cumulative growth calculations (KO ~11.5%, PEP ~8.7%) are arithmetically correct. The characterization of PEP's growth as 'front-loaded in FY2023' is supported: PEP added ~$5.1B in FY2023 vs. only ~$0.4B in FY2024 and ~$2.1B in FY2025. KO's 'slightly stronger compounding momentum' claim is directionally accurate in percentage terms.
missed_evidence: Neither analyst nor citations distinguish between organic/volume growth and pricing/FX effects, which are material for both companies in this period and would contextualize the headline revenue growth rates.

PEP's FY2025 revenue rebound ($93.9B vs. $91.9B in FY2024) is actually a meaningful acceleration after near-flat FY2024, which slightly undercuts the 'deceleration' narrative—the deceleration is real vs. FY2023 but FY2025 shows re-acceleration.

profitability.notes: All dollar figures are confirmed: KO GrossProfit FY2022 $25.0B / FY2025 $29.5B; PEP GrossProfit FY2025 $50.9B; KO NetIncome FY2025 $13.1B; PEP NetIncome FY2024 $9.6B / FY2025 $8.2B; KO OperatingIncome FY2024 $10.0B / FY2025 $13.8B; PEP OperatingIncome FY2024 $12.9B / FY2025 $11.5B. Gross margin calculations (KO ~61.6%, PEP ~54.1%) are arithmetically correct. The prior rebuttals regarding non-recurring items for both KO and PEP have been acknowledged by the analyst. Filing evidence confirms that KO's FY2025 operating income includes refranchising gains and gains on equity securities sales (e.g., $331M gain on CCEP stake sale, $102M refranchising gain, $409M in securities gains), supporting the prior rebuttal that the ~38% YoY surge is not purely operational. PEP's FY2025 filing confirms material impairment of intangible assets (Rockstar brand: $251M in EMEA; TBG: $19M; merger/integration impairments: $50M) and restructuring charges, supporting the prior rebuttal that PEP's net income decline is partly driven by non-recurring charges rather than pure margin compression.
missed_evidence: KO's FY2025 MDA explicitly references a $331M gain on partial sale of CCEP stake, $409M in securities gains, and $102M refranchising gains—these are material non-recurring items inflating both operating and net income that the narrative still does not quantify even after the rebuttal.

PEP's FY2025 filing shows impairment of intangible assets line items (Rockstar $251M, TBG $19M, merger-related $50M) and restructuring charges that materially depress reported operating and net income; the revised narrative acknowledges the risk but does not cite or quantify these specific items.

PEP's FY2024 operating income of $12.9B included only $33M in intangible impairments vs. materially higher charges in FY2025, making the year-over-year operating income comparison ($12.9B → $11.5B) heavily influenced by non-recurring items rather than underlying operational deterioration.

KO's net income in FY2023 ($10.7B) and FY2024 ($10.6B) were relatively stable, making the FY2025 jump to $13.1B even more anomalous and warranting explicit disclosure of the non-recurring drivers in the narrative.

cross_axis: Both axes rely on GAAP headline figures without adjusting for non-recurring items. The growth axis is less affected, but the profitability axis conclusions about 'superior margin quality' (KO) and 'margin compression' (PEP) are materially influenced by non-recurring items in both directions—KO's margins are flattered by asset sale gains and refranchising, while PEP's are depressed by brand impairments and restructuring. A core/adjusted profitability comparison would likely show a narrower divergence than the GAAP figures suggest.

The growth axis notes PEP's 'deceleration after FY2023,' but the profitability axis does not connect this to PEP's cost structure or the impairment charges that began appearing in FY2024–FY2025, which together explain both the revenue stagnation and margin pressure as potentially linked to portfolio rationalization (e.g., Rockstar, Be & Cheery brand write-downs).

claims.claim: Both KO and PEP have posted steady top-line growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, though at different scales and rates. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], representing cumulative growth of roughly 11.5% over three years, with each year adding incrementally: $45.8B in FY2023 [KO:Q1], $47.1B in FY2024 [KO:Q2], and $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3]. PEP, operating at roughly twice KO's revenue base, grew from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], a cumulative gain of about 8.7%, with the bulk of that growth front-loaded in FY2023 ($91.5B [PEP:Q1]) and only modest incremental gains in FY2024 ($91.9B [PEP:Q2]) and FY2025 ($93.9B [PEP:Q3]). In percentage terms, KO has demonstrated slightly stronger compounding momentum across this window, while PEP's growth has decelerated noticeably after FY2023.

PEP, operating at roughly twice KO's revenue base, grew from $86.4B in FY2022 [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B in FY2025 [PEP:Q3], a cumulative gain of about 8.7%, with the bulk of that growth front-loaded in FY2023 ($91.5B [PEP:Q1]) and only modest incremental gains in FY2024 ($91.9B [PEP:Q2]) and FY2025 ($93.9B [PEP:Q3]). In percentage terms, KO has demonstrated slightly stronger compounding momentum across this window, while PEP's growth has decelerated noticeably after FY2023.

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) both show revenue growth over the FY2022–FY2025 period, but their profitability trajectories diverge sharply in the most recent year. KO grew revenues from $43.0B in FY2022 [KO:Q0] to $47.9B in FY2025 [KO:Q3], while PEP operates at a much larger scale, expanding from $86.4B [PEP:Q0] to $93.9B [PEP:Q3]. On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

On gross margin, KO's gross profit rose from $25.0B [KO:Q12] to $29.5B [KO:Q15], implying a gross margin of roughly 61.6% in FY2025—meaningfully higher than PEP's $50.9B gross profit [PEP:Q15] on $93.9B in revenue, or about 54.1%, reflecting PEP's heavier exposure to lower-margin food and beverage manufacturing. The most striking divergence is at the bottom line in FY2025: KO's net income surged to $13.1B [KO:Q7] (a net margin of ~27.3%) while PEP's net income fell to $8.2B [PEP:Q7] (a net margin of ~8.8%), a sharp reversal from PEP's $9.6B in FY2024 [PEP:Q6]. Operating income tells a similar story—KO's operating income jumped to $13.8B in FY2025 [KO:Q11] after a dip to $10.0B in FY2024 [KO:Q10], whereas PEP's operating income declined to $11.5B in FY2025 [PEP:Q11] from a peak of $12.9B in FY2024 [PEP:Q10]. Overall, KO demonstrates superior margin quality and a strong FY2025 earnings rebound, while PEP faces margin compression despite its larger revenue base.

quality.notes: Exceptionally well-structured report. Revenue figures are consistently cited with source tags throughout. The growth section cleanly contrasts KO's steady compounding (~11.5%) against PEP's front-loaded then plateauing trajectory (~8.7%), with precise year-by-year anchoring. The profitability section is particularly strong: it correctly flags non-recurring items on both sides (KO's $800M+ in asset/securities/refranchising gains, PEP's impairments and restructuring charges) and explicitly cautions against reading headline figures as structural baselines—a mark of genuine analytical honesty. Gross margin differential (61.6% vs 54.1%) is correctly attributed to business model differences (concentrate vs. manufacturing-intensive). The only minor observation is that the Cross-Axis Takeaway section appears truncated in the submission, but the two completed sections are of high quality. No unsupported claims detected; uncertainty is appropriately flagged where full decomposition of non-recurring items is pending.
